texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Zeolites are crystallized aluminosilicate with well-de fi ned micropore (< 1 nm), and have been widely fabricated as membranes for gas separation. In the past decades, several types of zeolite membranes have been developed for gas separation, such as T-type [1,2], MFI [3,4], SAPO-34 [5,6], FAU [7], AlPO-18 [8] and DDR [9,10]. DDR zeolite membrane is attracting wide attentions due to its unique characteristics. The pure silica structure ensures it strong thermochemical and hydrothermal stabilities. The small open cage connected by 8-ring window (0.36×0.44 nm) allows accessible passage for small guest molecules, which can be used for CO2 separation and organic dehydration.

texts.orig: A B S T R A C T
texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A dilute synthesis precursor was developed to prepare high-quality DDR zeolite membranes on α -Al2O3 fourchannel hollow fi bers by secondary growth method. However, the as-membranes were susceptible to crack during high temperature activation (> 873 K) for the template removal. A molecular simulation demonstrated that a large contraction of ~ 5% occurred for the DDR zeolite framework after template removal. Ozone environment was employed for membranes activation at low temperature (473 K) to prevent the formation of cracks. The occluded template in zeolite cages was successfully removed by this way and high quality DDR zeolite membranes were achieved reproducibly without any cracks. The membranes were further evaluated for single gas permeation and separation of CO2/CH4 mixture. The ideal selectivities for CO2/CH4, CO2/N2, CO2/ C3H8, CO2/H2, N2/CH4 and O2/N2 were 305, 32, 1500, 5, 10 and 1.7, respectively. For the separation of equimolar CO2/CH4 mixture, a high selectivity of 500 and CO2 permeance of 3.5×10 -8 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 could be achieved. The membranes also exhibited high stability in wet environment. A stable permeance of 3.5×10 -8 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 and selectivity of 140 could be achieved during 40 h test for the separation of CO2/CH4 mixture containing 1.5% vapor at 353 K.

Based on molecular sieving, DDR membranes also show high separation selectivity for pervaporation dehydration regardless of the strong hydrophobicity of the membrane material. Kuhn et al. The research results suggested a great potential for DDR zeolite membranes in gas separation and organic dehydration [9 -13]. However, it seems a challenge to synthesize high quality DDR zeolite membranes. Zheng et al. [14] prepared the DDR membrane by in-situ crystallization and achieved a low selectivity factor of 2.4 for H2/CO2 at 297 K. Kim et al. [15] used secondary growth method to synthesize DDR membrane, which exhibited a maximum selectivity of 1.3 for CO2/ N2. Bose et al. [16] also employed a sonication-mediated hydrothermal technique in the synthesis of DDR membrane, and the selectivity was 2.8 for H2/CO2 at ambient temperature.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: seriously, cracks or intergranular defects were readily formed during high temperature activation of DDR zeolite membranes. Yang et al. [19] found that DDR membrane layer was very susceptible to crack after template removal at high temperatures, resulting in a low selectivity of 0.6 for CO2/CH4. They suggested a liquid phase chemical deposition to heal the cracks. Similarly, Kanezashi et al. [20] adopted chemical vapor deposition to reduce intercrystalline defects of DDR zeolite membranes formed during high temperature activation. Generally, 1-adamantanamine (ADA) with cage structure has been used as template to prepare DDR zeolite membranes, which requires high activation temperatures (>873 K) to decompose. Since the template is strictly con fi ned by pore windows of DDR zeolite, structure change would occur in the zeolite crystals after template removal. It can be judged from the variation of X-ray di ff raction pattern for DDR zeolite crystals before and after template removal [21]. Thus, high temperature thermal activation could cause serious cracks on membrane layer due to large expansion mismatching between membrane layer and substrate. We expected that the cracks could be avoided by reduction of expansion mismatching at low activation temperatures.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ozonication technique has been found an e ff ective method to eliminate organic template at low activation temperature with the assistance of high oxidability of ozone. Heng et al. [22] applied ozone to remove the template of MFI zeolite membranes, while the activation temperature could be controlled at 473 K. In this work, we attempted to adopt ozonication technique to remove the template of DDR zeolite membranes at low temperature. The membranes were hydrothermally synthesized on robust α -Al2O3 four-channel hollow fi ber supports developed in our laboratory [23]. Previously, we have adopted ball-milled Sigma-1 seeds to induce DDR zeolite crystals, and a short crystallization time of 9 h was needed [24]. Here, we further use the ball-milled Sigma-1 seeds to induce DDR zeolite membranes. Defect-free DDR zeolite membranes could be prepared by the low-temperature activation, showing ultra-high separation selectivity for CO2/CH4 separation.

texts.text: 2. Experiment

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1. Preparation of Sigma-1 zeolite
texts.text: 2.1. Preparation of Sigma-1 zeolite

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Sigma-1 zeolite crystals were synthesized according to our previous work [24]. The synthesis precursor was prepared by mixing 1-adamantanamine (ADA, Sigma-Aldrich), colloidal silica (Ludox SM-30, Sigma-Aldrich), sodium aluminate (NaAlO2, Alfa), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, Sigma-Aldrich) and deionized water, with a molar ratio of ADA: SiO2: Al2O3: Na2O: H2O = 20: 60: 2: 3: 2400. The precursor was hydrothermally crystallized at 453 K for 5 days in a rotary stainless steel autoclave. The as-synthesized Sigma-1 particles were washed with distilled water and dried overnight. The collected crystals showed circular disk shapes with a diameter of ~ 2 µm. Before used as seeds, the particles were ball-milled in aqueous medium for 2 h and ground to a mean particle size of 500 nm.
texts.text: Sigma-1 zeolite crystals were synthesized according to our previous work [24]. The synthesis precursor was prepared by mixing 1-adamantanamine (ADA, Sigma-Aldrich), colloidal silica (Ludox SM-30, Sigma-Aldrich), sodium aluminate (NaAlO2, Alfa), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, Sigma-Aldrich) and deionized water, with a molar ratio of ADA: SiO2: Al2O3: Na2O: H2O = 20: 60: 2: 3: 2400. The precursor was hydrothermally crystallized at 453 K for 5 days in a rotary stainless steel autoclave. The as-synthesized Sigma-1 particles were washed with distilled water and dried overnight. The collected crystals showed circular disk shapes with a diameter of ~ 2 µm. Before used as seeds, the particles were ball-milled in aqueous medium for 2 h and ground to a mean particle size of 500 nm.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2. Preparation of DDR zeolite membrane
texts.text: 2.2. Preparation of DDR zeolite membrane

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The DDR zeolite membrane was hydrothermally fabricated on the outer surface of home-made α -Al2O3 four-channel hollow fi ber supports by secondary growth. The detailed preparation process of four-channel hollow fi ber supports was reported in our previous work [23]. The hollow fi ber supports had an outer diameter of 3.6 mm and four cylindrical channels with the same inner diameter of 0.9 mm. The average pore size and porosity of the supports were ~ 0.5 µm and ~ 40%, respectively. Hollow fi ber supports with 70 mm long were fi rst coated with ball-milled Sigma-1 seeds. The seeds had an average particle size of ~ 500 nm. For seeding operation, both ends of hollow fi ber supports were wrapped with Te fl on tape, and the supports were then immersed vertically into an aqueous seeds suspension (1 wt%) for 15 s. The seeded supports were fi nally dried at 373 K overnight.
texts.text: The DDR zeolite membrane was hydrothermally fabricated on the outer surface of home-made α -Al2O3 four-channel hollow fi ber supports by secondary growth. The detailed preparation process of four-channel hollow fi ber supports was reported in our previous work [23]. The hollow fi ber supports had an outer diameter of 3.6 mm and four cylindrical channels with the same inner diameter of 0.9 mm. The average pore size and porosity of the supports were ~ 0.5 µm and ~ 40%, respectively. Hollow fi ber supports with 70 mm long were fi rst coated with ball-milled Sigma-1 seeds. The seeds had an average particle size of ~ 500 nm. For seeding operation, both ends of hollow fi ber supports were wrapped with Te fl on tape, and the supports were then immersed vertically into an aqueous seeds suspension (1 wt%) for 15 s. The seeded supports were fi nally dried at 373 K overnight.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The synthesis precursor was prepared by mixing ADA, colloidal silica, ethylenediamine (EDA, Sigma-Aldrich), and deionized water. Two recipes were used for synthesis of DDR zeolite membranes. The recipe I was referred to literature [9], which had a molar composition of ADA: SiO2: EDA: H2O = 9: 100: 150: 4000. The recipe II developed in our laboratory had a molar ratio of ADA: SiO2: EDA: H2O = 3: 100: 50: 4000. In the experimental procedure, ADA was fi rst dispersed in EDA/ water mixture at room temperature. For recipe I, the mixture was shaken at 368 K for 1 h to get a uniform solution; for recipe II, a homogenous mixture of ADA, EDA and D.I. water could be easily obtained by stirring at room temperature (298 K). Next, colloidal silica was dropwise added into the mixture under the continuous stirring. The synthesis mixture was aged for one hour under stirring at room temperature (298 K). For hythermal synthesis, a seeded support was fi rst placed vertically into a Te fl on-lined autoclave with an internal volume of 50 mL. A 40 mL synthesis mixture was poured into the autoclave, which was then placed statically in an oven. The hydrothermal crystallization was carried out at 403 -423 K for 48 h. After cooling down, the membranes were removed from the autoclaves and rinsed with DI water several times. The membranes were fi nally dried at 333 K overnight.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3. Template removal
texts.text: 2.3. Template removal

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Prior to membrane activation, DDR zeolite membranes were applied for helium permeance test to probe any non-zeolitic pores. The membranes with a helium permeance of < 10 -10 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 were placed in a stainless steel module sealed by graphite O-ring, which was put in a programmable oven. Dry oxygen containing 80 g m -3 ozone (O3/O2) was then introduced into the module at a fl ow rate of 300 cm 3 min -1 , where the ozone was produced by an electrical discharge generator (Jiuzhoulong, JZCF-G-3). The organic template occluded in the zeolitic pores was oxidized in the ozone environment at 473 K. The heating and cooling rates were controlled at 1 K min -1 . For comparison, air environment was also used to activate DDR zeolite membranes at 473 K and 923 K, respectively.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A DDR zeolite membrane was mounted in a stainless steel module, with each end sealed by a silicone O-ring. The membrane module was then placed in a tubular furnace for gas permeation. The feed pressure was modulated by a back pressure regulator, and the permeate side was operated under atmospheric pressure. Single gas permeation was measured in a dead-end mode and the gas fl ow rate was measured by a bubble fl ow meter. For separation of binary CO2/CH4 mixture, an equimolar fl ow was fed at a rate of about 800 mL min -1 , and a helium stream (50 mL min -1 ) was used to sweep the permeate side. A gas chromatography (GC, GC-2014, Shimadzu), equipped with thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and a packed column of HAYESEP-DB 200, was used to analyze gas compositions. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.6. Molecular simulation
texts.text: 2.6. Molecular simulation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Molecular simulation was used to evaluate the lattice parameters of the DDR zeolite crystal cell during the template removal. It was carried out by the ' Discovery ' module in ' Material Studio ' software using a COMPASS force fi eld, which is particularly developed for zeolites according to molecular mechanics. The energy optimization process was conducted using the smart minimizer method installed in the software package. In the simulation, a 2×2×1 (1140-atom) supercell of DDR zeolite was created without imposing any restrictions on the lattice vectors and atomic coordinates, and then various ADA molecules were arbitrarily dosed in the zeolite cages, where atomic relaxation can be fully adjusted with the supercell structure during the geometry optimization.

texts.orig: 3.1. Characterization of DDR zeolite membrane
texts.text: 3.1. Characterization of DDR zeolite membrane

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We fi rst prepared DDR membranes following the synthesis approach (recipe I) referred to literature [9]. The solution obtained in this approach is milk-like at room temperature (Fig. 1a), and a high temperature oil bath (368 K) was required in the synthesis process of precursor. This led to segregated template phase from the solution bulk and the vaporized EDA solvent under oil heating. It seemed di ffi cult to control molar ratio of the synthesis precursor accurately. This phenomenon is mainly responsible for the poor repeatability of the high quality of the DDR membrane.
texts.text: We fi rst prepared DDR membranes following the synthesis approach (recipe I) referred to literature [9]. The solution obtained in this approach is milk-like at room temperature (Fig. 1a), and a high temperature oil bath (368 K) was required in the synthesis process of precursor. This led to segregated template phase from the solution bulk and the vaporized EDA solvent under oil heating. It seemed di ffi cult to control molar ratio of the synthesis precursor accurately. This phenomenon is mainly responsible for the poor repeatability of the high quality of the DDR membrane.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To improve membrane repeatability, we developed a dilute synthesis solution (recipe II) for DDR membrane fabrication in our laboratory. As seen from Fig. 1b, the solution prepared by the recipe II is clear at room temperature. By reducing template amount, the solution is completely dissolvable at room temperature and the oil heating step is omitted, which save the experimental cost considerably and simplify the fabrication process. As seen from Fig. 1b, the solution prepared by the recipe II is clear at room temperature. By reducing template amount, the solution is completely dissolvable at room temperature and the oil heating step is omitted, which save the experimental cost considerably and simplify the fabrication process. A homogeneous and clear solution is easily obtained at room temperature via the dilute synthesis precursor. In the following work, we thus adopted the recipe II for further membrane

texts.orig: Fig. 3a and b show SEM images for cross-section and seeded surface of four-channel hollow fi ber support. A uniform seed layer containing ball-milled seeds was coated on the support surface, which was helpful for formation of continuous membrane layer. Fig. 3c-f show SEM images of the DDR zeolite membrane synthesized at di ff erent temperature. It is evident that the membrane morphology is strongly dependent on synthesis conditions. As shown in Fig. 3c, a relatively low synthesis temperature ( T =403 K) led to low crystallinity and big intercrystalline defects in the membrane layer. In contrast, a relatively high temperature ( T =423 K) could result in the presence of impurity SGT zeolite in the membrane layer (Fig. 3d). Typical rhombus-shaped DDR zeolite crystals with well intergrowth could be formed in the membrane layer at the crystallization temperature of 413 K (Fig. 3e-f). The size of DDR crystals was about 2 -4 µm and the thickness of the membrane layer was about 5 -8 µm. The as-synthesized DDR zeolite membranes were also evaluated by single gas N2 permeation before template removal. It was found that N2 permeace through the membranes synthesized for more than 48 h was less than 10 -10 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 , indicating high quality of membrane layers were formed on the Al2O3 substrates. The membranes synthesized at 413 K for 48 h were further adopted for activations and separation

texts.orig: To eliminate the ADA occluded in the zeolite cages, we fi rst activated the membranes at a temperature of 923 K in air. The high temperature treatment method has been commonly used for template removal of DDR zeolite membranes [9,19]. Unfortunately, the activated membranes showed very high N2 permeance of more than 5×10 -7 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 , implying that defects could be generated in the membrane layers after the thermal treatment. The SEM images in Fig. 4a showed that big cracks with the width of lager than 20 nm were formed in the membrane surface. We checked the preparation procedure carefully and found that the cracks were generated in the activation step. When using an activated membrane for separation of equimolar CO2/CH4 mixtures, an extremely low selectivity factor of 0.5 was achieved. The result suggested that the cracks were fatal to the separation performance of DDR zeolite membranes. The formation of cracks was also reported in literature [19]. We failed to address the issue by reduce the heating and cooling rates during the activation process.

texts.orig: It was documented that the cracks in MFI zeolite membranes were predominately attributed to a mismatching of the thermal expansion between the support and zeolite layer [25]. However, we were curious about why the mismatch after template removal was so big as to cause the fatal cracks for DDR zeolite membranes. Therefore, molecular simulation based on a COMPASS force fi eld was employed to explore the in fl uence of the template on the lattice parameters of the DDR zeolite. The results are summarized in Table 1. It can be seen that the absence of ADA molecule in the DDR cells not only caused the changes of lattice angles, but also the extension of lattice lengths. A total volume contraction of ~ 5% was derived for the template removal. In addition, the volume thermal expansion coe ffi cients at high and low temperature were quite di ff erent for DDR zeolite. texts.orig: 492 K and 1185 K, a negative value of -8.7×10 -6 /K was obtained [26], while at a low temperature between 298 K and 492 K, the value was derived to be 35.1×10 -6 /K. During temperature rising stage from room temperature to 923 K, DDR zeolite membrane layer underwent a volume expansion and contraction while the Al2O3 substrate expanded continuously with a coe ffi cient of about 7.5×10 -6 /K. This would build up more strains in the membrane layer before template oxidation [27]. As a result, the extra contraction of DDR zeolite caused by template removal resulted in big cracks in the membrane layer. At a low temperature such as 473 K, the contraction caused by template removal could be compensated by thermal expansion of DDR zeolite. Therefore, it is possible to avoid generation of cracks if the template is eliminated at low temperature.

texts.orig: In this work, we used a gas environment of highly oxidative ozone to activate the DDR membranes at a low temperature of 473 K. As shown in Fig. 4b, no cracks occurred in the membrane surface after activation in mixture of O3/O2 at 473 K for 60 h. We adopted N2 permeation to examine whether the template was e ff ectively removed. Fig. 5 shows the N2 permeance of the membrane activated in mixture of O3/O2 for di ff erent duration time. As can be seen, the duration time below 40 h was insu ffi cient to open the pores of DDR zeolite membranes. While a stable fl ux could be obtained when the activation time was longer than 60 h. For the purpose of comparison, oxygen was adopted to activate the membrane at low temperature of 473 K for 80 h. However, the activated membrane was still gas-tight for N2. The above results suggested that the template in DDR zeolite cages could be eliminated at low temperature with the assistance of ozone.

texts.orig: Fig. 6 shows XRD patterns of DDR zeolite membranes activated by O3/O2 with di ff erent activated time. The intensity of characteristic peak at 2 θ = 6.5 was obviously intensi fi ed after template removal, which was consistent with the result in literature [21]. It was suggested that the structure change occurred in DDR zeolite crystals after template removal. As shown in Fig. 6, it can be seen that the intensity of characteristic peak at 2 θ =6.5 became stronger as the activation time was extended from 0 h to 80 h under ozone atmosphere, indicating that the removal of template was in progress.

texts.orig: membrane with di ff erent thermal activation duration at 473 K. Fig. 7 illustrates the separation results of CO2/CH4 over membrane at the activation period from 25 h to 100 h. As shown in the fi gure, the membrane exhibited CO2 selective after thermal activation for ca. 40 h. However, the CO2 permeance was very low. With extension of activation time from 40 h to 80 h, CO2 permeance through DDR zeolite membrane increased continuously while CH4 permeance increased slowly. This led to a gradual increase in separation factor for CO2/CH4. The result suggested that the zeolitic pores of the membrane were opened gradually, which gave more channels for O3 to access the occluded template. For an activation time of 80 h, the membrane exhibited a separation factor of 500 and CO2 permeance of 3.5×10 -8 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 . After the activation time exceeded 80 h, the permeance and separation selectivity tended to be stable, suggesting that the occluded template were completely removed and all the zeolitic pores were opened. TGA and DSC were further used to determine the membrane after 80 h of activation, and no exothermic peak was detected, thereby con fi rming the complete oxidation of template [28] (data not shown here). We prepared fi ve membranes using the same approach. The separation results for CO2/CH4 are shown in Table 2. As can be seen, all the membranes had high separation selectivity of CO2/CH4 in the range of 350 -500. It was thus suggested that the dilute synthesis precursor had high reproducibility for producing dense DDR zeolite membranes. More importantly, the low-temperature activation could avoid the generation of cracks in DDR zeolite membrane layer.

Basically, the permeance decreased with increase of kinetic diameter for those gases, indicating that activated di ff usion dominates gas di ff usion. Table 3 shows the ideal selectivity of DDR zeolite membrane for di ff erent gases. As can be seen, the as-synthesis DDR membrane exhibit high selectivities for CO2/CH4, CO2/N2 and CO2/C3H8, which was due to high contribution of surface di ff usion for CO2 permeation. Even though CO2 is bigger than H2, the ideal selectivity for CO2/H2 was about 5. Since O2, N2 and CH4 have close molecular sizes and low a ffi nity to DDR zeolite, the separations of N2/CH4 and O2/N2 mainly depend on molecular sieving. As can be seen, N2/CH4 (10) has higher ideal selectivity than O2/N2 (1.7), which suggested that the actual pore size of DDR zeolite membrane was located between molecular sizes of N2 and CH4. Furthermore, all the separation selectivities in Table 3 are greater than their Knudsen factors, indicating DDR zeolite membranes have promising applications in separation of light gases.

texts.orig: Fig. 10 shows the CO2/CH4 separation results as a function of feed pressure as operated at 298 K. When the pressure increased from 0.1 MPa to 0.5 MPa, the CO2 permeance decreased from 5×10 -8 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 to 3×10 -8 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 while CH4 permeance increased slightly, which led to a decrease in selectivity from 420 to 190. The results could be explained as follows. Although higher partial pressure enhanced adsorption of CO2, the intermolecular interaction was enhanced as well due to more molecules adsorbed in the zeolite cages, which reduced gas di ff usivity [30 -32]. For CH4, the gas adsorption capacity was much smaller and far below the saturation, and thus any increases of partial pressure only enhanced the adsorption near the pore window, thereby increasing the di ff usivity.

texts.orig: Table 4 illustrates CO2/CH4 separation results though DDR zeolite membranes for M2 and those reported in literature [9 -11,19,29]. The synthesis conditions for those membranes were also presented in the Table 4. As can be seen, the DDR membranes prepared in this work showed a considerably high selectivity, which could be due to the low temperature activation. It should be noted that our preparation approach has high reproducibility. The relatively lower CO2 permeance could be related with the dense membrane layer by low temperature activation. Compared with the DDR membranes provided by NGK insulators [9 -11,29], we can prepare high quality membranes induced with Sigma-1 seeds that could be prepared in short synthesis time. In addition, the dilute synthesis precursor was adopted with less amount of template, which makes fabrication operation more easily and economically.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For puri fi cation of natural gas, a certain amount of water vapor inevitably exists in the separation system, which has an e ff ect on the separation performance of DDR zeolite membranes. In this work, we also used high-quality DDR zeolite membrane (M5) to evaluate the CO2/CH4 separation in moist environment. Fig. 11 depicted the separation results of M5 for dry and humidi fi ed CO2/CH4 mixtures with di ff erent temperature. A water vapor with a partial pressure of 4.5 kPa was contained in the moisture mixture. As can be seen, the introduction of water vapor caused a decline of separation performance for DDR zeolite membrane. For instance, at a low temperature of 303 K, CO2 permeance decreases from 3.6×10 -8 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 to 0.45×10 -8 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 , while the separation selectivity for CO2/CH4 decreased from 300 to 80. This was because the water vapor could be easily adsorbed in zeolite pores, and partially blocked the di ff usion passage for CO2 permeation. However, due to strong hydrophobicity of pure-silica DDR zeolite, the membranes exhibited better separation performance than SAPO-34 in moist environment as reported in literature [33,34]. With the increases in separation temperature, the adsorbed water in DDR zeolite pores was released continuously. As a result, the separation performance in the wet condition was gradually close to that in the dry condition. At 393 K, the CO2 permeance and separation selectivity were nearly similar to the value in the dry system, which were 1.1×10 -8 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 and 100, respectively. After removing water vapor and calcination at 393 K for 10 h, the membrane recovered to the previous separation performance in dry environment at the temperature range of 303 -393 K. The longterm stability of DDR membrane (M5) was also investigated for separation of wet CO2/CH4 mixture with 1.5% water vapor at 353 K, as shown in Fig. 12. During the 40 h test in wet system, the DDR zeolite membrane showed excellent stability, which had CO2 permeance and separation selectivity for CO2/CH4 of about 1.2×10 -8 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 and 140 respectively. The result suggested that the DDR zeolite membranes had a good water-tolerance in moist environment.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, we proposed a simple synthesis approach with less amount of template for preparation of high quality of DDR membranes induced by ball-milled Sigma-1 seeds. The removal of template from DDR zeolite could cause contraction of zeolite framework by ~ 5%. At high temperature, Al2O3 substrate expands with increase of temperature while the DDR zeolite membrane layer contracts. As a result, cracks could be readily generated at high activated temperature for template removal due to large expansion mismatch between membrane layer and support. However, the cracks could be e ff ectively avoided by thermal activation at low temperature of 473 K under ozone environment, because the contraction caused by template removal could be compensated by thermal expansion of DDR zeolite in the temperature range. The as-prepared DDR zeolite membranes exhibited high separation performance for CO2/CH4 separation, e.g. separation selectivity of 500 and CO2 permeance of 3.5×10 -8 mol m -2 s -1 Pa -1 at 298 K and feed pressure of 200 kPa. Moreover, the DDR zeolite membrane exhibited high stability for CO2/CH4 mixture under wet condition. The excellent separation performance suggests a promising application of DDR zeolite membranes in natural gas puri fi cation.
